metric_value: Both companies are rightly attacking the mobile PC space, which is the fastest-growing portion of the industry.
2003 proved that laptop sales are price elastic in that $800 to $1,000 laptops are flying off the shelves.

The Celeron M is not a 'dumbing down'; Intel's chip is simply a low-cost, high-performance microprocessor designed for the wireless world. It will be quickly incorporated into value-oriented, under-$1,000 machines.
AMD's Mobile Athlon 64, which is aimed at the same notebook market, is another respin of AMD's long-term effort to increase laptop market share.
Mercury Research president Dean McCarron: 2004 is probably going to be one of the more exciting years in microprocessors because of all the new products and the positioning.
Intel Celeron M pricing: 1.3 GHz $134, 1.2 GHz $107, 800 MHz ULV $161 (all in 1,000-unit quantities). 0.13-micron process; value-vs-Pentium-M differentiation.
AMD Mobile Athlon 64: industry's only Windows-compatible 64-bit mobile processor for mainstream notebooks; 60+ manufacturers and system builders supporting, including HP, eMachines, Fujitsu.

Sub-$1,000 laptop share grew from ~25% in 2003 to ~60% by 2010 per Gartner/IDC — strongly validating Kastner's 2004 price-elasticity thesis. Netbooks 2007-2011, sub-$500 Chromebooks 2011+, budget Windows laptops became the bulk of PC unit sales.
